replies.author: @spnked9516
replies.text:
I actually disagree with this sentiment.

See, you or I might care about the the quality of movies and the standards of storytelling, but the average person does not. The average person will "watch" anything, provided they associate some sense of mild amusement to, and provided nothing in it is particularly jarring. 

Now, I put the word watch in quotations because many people don't actually watch film and television, in a traditional sense. The modern incarnation of "watching" amounts to little more than background noise. People don't sit there and watch a movie anymore, they scroll on their phone, play a game, or otherwise spit their attention in some manner. Many films and TV shows are produced now with a predictable rhythm and signposting that lets the audience know when to look at the screen, all so that association of mild amusement an be achieved.

replies.author: @darthgorbag
replies.text:
@1stCallipostle As someone who strictly rented DVDs from Netflix up until they axed that service only a couple years ago, trust me they only 5% of the catalogue they used to have, AT BEST. I feel like I'm the only person the whole world who even knows this, even though I've been telling people ever since Netflix first introduced streaming. 
Their streaming catalogue only ever included a TINY fraction of the vast library of movies, tv shows, animes, documentaries, & more that were available from their DVD warehouses. Anything a person could think of that had ever been produced, there was a very good chance it was available on Netflix DVD. There were SO MANY good things I watched that I never even heard of before and never would have heard of at all if I hadn't seen them while scrolling through the Netflix website looking for more things to add to my DVD queues. It was one of the greatest services I ever knew.
But the minute Netflix announced their streaming feature, it was like the entire world just blissfully chose to forget that such as thing as DVDs ever existed. Any time there was any post or conversation on social media where NF DVDs were mentioned, there would always be comments from people saying "DVDs? What are those?" or "I totally forgot NF even did DVDs." 
Also people would often comment online that they want to watch a certain show or movie, but "it's not currently on NF" (especially back in the pre covid era when NF was still the only streaming service). I would look up that show to confirm, then reply to those people saying "Actually that show IS available on NF right now! ...on DVD. NF has 1000 times more selection of things in their DVD library than on their streaming library." But it was pointless. On the few occasions that people would reply back to me, they'd say what amounts to "I'm too lazy for physical media. I want instant gratification."

It really taught me that the human population cannot be persuaded to do things that are in their own best interests, even for the sake of their own pleasure. Humans, for the most part, are severely lazy, selfish, unintelligent, & apathetic. Convenience will ALWAYS win in the marketplace. People like us who care about all the other aspects of things, who care about the bigger picture, who want entertainment to be focused on quality, who want to collect physical media, who want history to be preserved, who want to be treated like intelligent adults... we are a micro-minority. We are far too small a niche to move the needles of economic & technological trends. We are a few professors drowned out in a vast sea of never-aging toddlers.

The critical drinker repeatedly has commented on the cinema experience. But I think it's incorrect and mostly reflects that he has a big TV at home and not a lot of light issues. Well done! Currently the choice is between watching film in the cinema or at home. However, once netflix wins, are films going to be films?

A question: Is netflix good? They've been making original films for 10 years. People talk about second screen viewing and dumbing down plots. OK. A different measure would observing that they're the dominant player but their 10 year record isn't of making genre defining cultural touchstones. Offhand, I can't remember a netflix film that had the importance of 2001, A clockwork orange, The Matrix, Pulp Fiction, Full Metal Jacket, The Ascent, Apocalypse Now, Citizen Kane, The Godfather, ET, Schindler's Lists, The Deer Hunter, Taxi Driver, Jaws, Fight Club, Blade Runner, Alien, or even Birth of a Nation (racist, but accomplished for its day). Is netflix good? If netflix is the dominant force and capable of art, then they should be able to put together a ten year period that rivals an average or even a good 10 year period from UA, WB, Miramax. Maybe they can buy up some existing talent, but can they foster new talent? Do they have instincts on emerging filmmakers to take a chance on? Do they have people willing to tell Scorcese that his film is 60 minutes too long?

The choice is presented between watching a film in a cinema or at home. But if Disney and Netflix corrode every studio they purchase, then the choice is between watching having cinemas (and films) versus having content. Netflix did fork out massive cash for some classical directors. But then funded Damsel and Electric State (which supposedly cost 320 million).

author: @CriticalNobody
text:
I’ll never forget seeing RDJ Sherlock Holmes in theaters. Some lady a few rows in front of me was yapping on her phone during the previews but no one said anything. She didn’t even stop when the movie started; talking at full volume in a crowded theater as the opening scene started. Once again, everyone kept quiet…except for one brave man who told her to shut up. Her response was to yell at him to mind his own business. An argument started and every other person in the theater just kept quiet and hoped it would stop.

Shortly after, I saw a shooting star fly through the darkness and crash into the wall. The man had snatched her phone away and chucked it as hard as he could. The woman and the friend she was with lost it, started screaming at him and got up to leave. We all cheered as they stormed out and watched the rest of the movie in peace.

replies.author: @justforever96
replies.text:
Well that kind of goes with having a population four times what it used to be. I grew up in a small town, we all knew who everyone was, where they lived, what their story was, we cared what happened and watched out for each other, and more or less policed ourselves. I don't really buy the idea of smell towns being "nosy", they are just aware and keep track of what everyone else does because you are all related to some extent and you all effect one another. But you can't really do that in a larger town, at best you might know the people on your street, but they come and they go. And we don't have actual communal events. Even the theater was kind of a last dying vestige of that. When did you actually talk to the people in your theater and get to know them? Whereas the the early 20th century in our towns, we didn't have theaters, or TVs, radio was still a pretty new and spotty thing. Party line telephones were one form of entertainment, listening in on other people's calls, it was just expected. But instead they had work bees, of a neighbor was installing a new carpet, or a new barn, or just had a ton of corn to shuck, they would invite the whole neighborhood over to help, and they will come. They would get fed a big meal, laugh and chat and work together, the young people would get a chance to meet with supervision, the work got done easily, everyone had fun and got fed, and next time you go help them out when they need it. Or they would just have a dance, where a family would empty out the ground floor, call in a fiddler, and you would have a big meal, some drinks, dance, everyone donated something to the fiddler, and went home happy. That's real community bonding, and we haven't had anything remotely like that for a long time. Cars ruined it, TV ruined it, the growing population ruined it, and now you probably live packed among other people but totally alone, while back then they lived in isolated farmhouses in small communities, with their own schoolhouse for their own local kids, but still felt known and like a part of a community. 
Call me old fashioned and backwards but in not convinced that we have a better system today. Some things were worse back then but im not sure what we lost was worth what we gained.

replies.author: @gwolks1008
replies.text:
​​@RC-gh7os We humans are good at pathetically looking back with nostalgia; presumably some preservation mechanism. It comforts us and makes us feel as though we had control or understanding of the world around us. That, in itself, is false in my opinion.

I reckon it is always a mere illusion. We cannot truly say if we live in better times today, 500 or 5000 years ago, because that would require examining a person's psyche, which we cannot do. You might end up discovering that way back when people were profoundly more happy or far less.

My recommendation - try not to understand it all. We want to decipher human behavior and run a logical thread through everything as if we are scientists seeking some higher truth. But, we are met with two big problems: 1. Human behavior contains irrationally; 2. Large systems develop hierarchical complexity that we stand no chance in really comprehending. 
As such, I believe that estimating how Good or Bad these times are is naive. We can only try to do some good for the people around us.

replies.author: @georgejones3526
replies.text:
@nicolobertollo3456 - There were speakers attached to cables that hung on metal poles placed between the cars and the speakers had volume controls. You would reach out of the car, bring the speaker inside and hang it on the top edge of your window. It was strictly monaural and not the best quality sound. Also, the ground where you parked was ridged up so that the car tilted upwards making it easier to see the giant screen. Near the center of the parking lot was a building that served snacks, popcorn and soft drinks. It was not high quality but it was convenient and fun, especially for teenagers. My wife and I saw Star Wars when it came out at a drive-in while our three month old son slept in the back seat. It would have been very difficult if not impossible to do at a regular theater.
I almost forgot. There was a collection basket by the exit for people who forgot to remove the speaker before driving off. 😊

replies.author: @justforever96
replies.text: Laundromats are available because not all people have a washing machine. Tons of rental units don't have one, because it takes up a bunch of space when you need it for living space, and expensive hookups, vents, etc. It's much easier for the landlord to just offer it without, instead of trying to fit four sets of washers and dryers into a building. Or 40 if it's a big place. I'm currently living without a washing machine for the first time in my life, I have no choice but to go to the laundromat, while I can easily get a large TV or just watch stuff on my phone if I really need to. My only option besides the laundromat is to find a friend who will let me use theirs, or wash my stuff by hand in the sink. I can't just install a washer because it's not my property. There are tens of millions of rental units in the US without washing machines, that's why laundromats still exist. People who don't have a TV do that by choice (that's me) and most of the people who still do the theaters have TVs at home, they just choose to go the theater anyway because it's a different experience. So those really aren't comparable situations. No one goes to a laundromat just because they enjoy it more than doing it at home in their own machine (unless they are paying to have someone wash their clothes), and not many people are going to theaters because they don't have any way to watch movies at home and can't get one.

Cinema has outlived every ‘death prophecy’ ever made. The only constant is that every generation believes they’re witnessing the end, when in reality they’re witnessing a transition.

text: I remember watching the OG Jurassic Park in the movie theater after it came out. It was so good. No cellphones, people still had respect for other people, and the movie itself was just amazing. It was just such an immersive experience and I loved every second of it. I miss movies like that. The way they could make something so out there (dinosaurs coming back) seem so real is a testament to how good everyone was at their jobs on that movie. We got there, took up two rows (clustered rather than spread out), and waited. One of the messages displayed before the film started was "Thank you for not talking during the movie. Some of us as teens understood the importance of a "stfu" environment during movie-viewing experiences.

Funny you mention that, because when he said you can get a decent home theater for about a grand, the first thing I thought was sure, the picture part is easy enough. Just get a decent, adequately large screen, Done. Literally one thing and that's it, and simply putting a screen in a room is pretty doable in practically any room layout.

But good audio? Not saying it's impossible, but it's way more complicated and takes way more parts and way more fiddling. Depending on room layout, finding good places to put all the directional speakers and manage to plug them all in and all that is a huge pain. Do you choose between non-ideal speaker location for listening purposes, or put it in the right spot, but have a power cord going across where people can trip on it? Then you turn it all on and for some reason the audio refuses to go out to the actual sound system speakers. Then you fix that and for some reason the dialog is way too quiet. Or the heavy bass kinda buzzes. And on and on. I've NEVER gotten good in-home audio figured out.

replies.author: @herrikudo
replies.text:
​@caspearious-ghost if you are actually asking I can explain. A monopoly does not necessarily mean you are the only player in the game. While yes the term itself means literally that, in common terminology people use it interchangeably with Duopoly, Triopoly, oligopoly, etc.

The reason this can be considered a monopoly, or more technically defined a Triopoly, is because netflix will now control a disproportionate share of the movie and streaming industry. Their competitors were HBO Max and Prime. Prime also controls an insane share of the movie industry. However, by purchasing WB, Netflix now owns HBO Max. Further reducing it to a functional Duopoly. Sure you have peacock and other smaller services, but the companies are starting to collaborate.

Historically the market being controlled by so few players has led to price gouging and other consumer abuses. As it currently stands, I have to pay a ridiculous amount of money for prime or netflix, only to recieve as many ads as I see on free services.

How much worse do you think this is gonna get now that one of the only threats to netflix is now owned by netflix, and their competitor is notoriously corrupt and corporacratic Amazon?

author: @louisekinsley718
text:
I agree with you, it is the beginning of the end. Ex (female) cinema projectionist here, of the now defunct ABC chain met my hubby through work and we loved film, our jobs. After me leaving out of choice and hubby changing his career in1998, watching cinemas change through the years. We saw it's decline 30 years ago and granted some might say 30 years is a long time but not to me. I saw multiplexes arrive and even worked at 2 multiplex chains for a while, which was heralded as the closure of traditional cinemas. To me this was the beginning of the end.

With 3 children, I should have taken them to the cinema, carried on the interest, I did somewhat. The arrival of digital ended my trained skill, projectionist being a dead career, film is rarely used now sadly and I ran some astounding perfect films on the BIG BIG screens of old, used to get the buzz of presenting gems to an audience, it was truly a special moment. I stopped going to the cinema 15 years ago and from afar watched it all go down the drain.

Sad but very very true, cinema is dying/dead.

replies.author: @chindleymuffin
replies.text:
Funnily enough the original versions of the original Star Wars Trilogy are due to come to cinemas next year, thanks to the work of the British Film Institute (the BFI) restoring the original copies they had in their vault (they were all filmed in the UK, apart from a couple of location shots in Tunisia for Tattooine in the first one, Norway for Hoth in Empire and Yellowstone Park for Endor in Jedi) and cleaning them up to modern 4K standards. 

All approved and overseen by Kathleen Kennedy (the only good thing she's done so far), I think they will be released next year just in time for the 50th Anniversary in 2027 and I assume eventually on 4K Blu Ray as a 50th Anniversary Edition.

Hang in there! No one acknowledges how hard it can be for dads. Our girls have the best father but he feels overwhelmed and rejected a lot of the time. He’s a wonderful husband, too, and I try to tell him that as much as I can, but a lot of people aren’t wired/raised in a way that it comes naturally to say it as often as people really need to hear it.

If you can, ask for help- if you don’t have family nearby or can’t afford a babysitter, see if you can make a deal with some other couple to hang onto each other’s kids so you can each get a few hours together from time to time. At a certain point we had to double down on protecting some time for just the two of us. Not sure how old your kids are, but it’s slowly getting better as our kids move out of their toddler years and are more self-sufficient, fun and (occasionally) appreciative.
